dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a headache call at a business in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Michael and I'm an EMT. What symptoms are you having today?
He's been speaking strangely for the past hour.
Has the patient experienced anything like this in the past?
No, only this odd speech and a bad headache. He hasn't been right since early morning.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me what your name is?
Thomas Smith.
Can you tell me the year right now?
2020, I think.
Thomas, where are you at this moment?
I think I'm at work.
Good. Our name is Thomas, and we are at work. Can you tell me what was going on earlier?
I don't remember. I feel confused.
Eyes open with normal reactivity, no signs of altered responsiveness.
Eye opening is spontaneously occurring and tracking appropriately.
Verbal response is coherent except for occasional difficulty with articulation.
Mr. Smith, can you grip my fingers tightly?
Says "Okay" and demonstrates equal grip strength in both hands.
Airway is patent without signs of obstruction.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respiratory rate is 20, depth unspecified but appears rhythmic.
Are you having any trouble breathing or chest discomfort?
No difficulty. Just headache and trouble speaking.
Breath sounds are bilaterally clear, no wheezes or crackles noted.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Pulse is 77, regular and centrally palpable.
Blood pressure is currently 176 over 108.
Skin is cool, dry, and pale with no signs of cyanosis.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Was there anything unusual before the symptoms started?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: No unusual activities. Last seen normal at 8:30 AM this morning.
Any past conditions or surgeries?
He has a history of hypertension and traumatic brain injury.
What medications are you on?
He takes Clonidine for hypertension daily.
Do you have any known allergies?
No known allergies.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally.
Noting a line of redness at fifth intercostal space, skin warm above, cool and pale below.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth, and throat - Expression capability; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Try to repeat these words: "test, assist, pressure."

dialogue.role: nose, mouth, and throat - Expression capability; Patient
dialogue.utterance: Says "test, assist, maybe pressure next time."
Heart sounds are regular without murmur.

dialogue.micro_intent: GCS exam - motor and verbal function
dialogue.utterance: GCS score is 14, with verbal and motor function intact but delayed.
Blood glucose initially at 113 mg/dL.
Blood pressure is currently 163 over 102.
Oxygen saturation remains stable at 97%.
EKG strips show the patient in sinus rhythm.
Respiratory rate unchanged at 18.
Initiating 4-lead EKG as part of protocol.
4-lead confirm rhythm, proceeding with 12-lead EKG now.
12-lead EKG reveals findings consistent with posterior-anterolateral infarct.
Performed Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale - no clinical signs of stroke seen initially.
Patient denies any pain on assessment.
IV started in left antecubital without complication.
250 ml of warmed normal saline administered per protocol for CVA syndromes.
Field impression is stroke/TIA, activating protocol 3 per standing orders.
Completed stroke scale a second time-still negative for stroke signs.
Repeat assessment shows a 78, regular pulse.
Resp still 18, with no added use of accessory muscles.
Blood pressure now reads 165 over 94.
Oxygen saturation measures at 95%.
Patient has a lower than normal temperature.
Final pulse reading 75, regular.
Recheck BP unchanged at 165 over 94.
Second glucose reading is non-recordable or 0 mg/dL.
Noting periorbital and bilateral lower and upper limb edema.
Patient is transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for neurological imaging.
Patient seated in semi-Fowler's position using five-point harness.
Five-point harness used during move from stretcher to ER bed.
Male, 48, field impression stroke/TIA. 12-lead suggests posterior-anterolateral infarct, likely CVA.
Hypotonic hands, normal IV access, patient denies pain, and glucose inconsistent.
